title: Needle-felted miniature hedgehog
subtitle: A small hedgehog figure in brown and cream merino with textured spine surface
excerpt: A needle-felted hedgehog is built from a single pear-shaped body with two distinct zones: a smooth cream belly and face, and a dark brown back textured to suggest spines. The project introduces surface texture work and the two-tone body technique. Finished size is approximately 6 cm long.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted hedgehog figures are a common subject in British craft instruction. Technique is observation-led.

content.text: A hedgehog body is a flattened pear shape: wider at the haunches, tapering to a pointed nose. The cream belly and face area covers the lower half and the nose end. The dark brown back covers the upper half.
creates the boundary between the two zones. The
is applied to the dark brown surface after the body is fully shaped.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out. Use a foam pad at least 4 cm thick; never work over a hard surface.
attrs.title: Needle safety

items.name: Merino roving, black (eyes and nose tip)
Dense foam pad, 4 cm thick minimum

content.text: Mix equal amounts of dark brown and cream roving and form a ball. Needle into a flattened pear shape, about 6 cm long and 4 cm wide at the back end. Flatten the base slightly so the hedgehog sits level without support.

Lay a thin wisp of cream roving across the bottom half of the body (the belly) and over the nose end. Needle flat with the medium needle. The cream should cover approximately the lower third of the body surface and the nose and cheeks. Blend the upper boundary of the cream into the body with light needling so there is no hard line.
Step 3: Apply the dark brown spine zone
Lay a layer of dark brown roving across the upper back surface. Needle firmly with the medium needle until the layer is smooth and compact.

Using the coarse needle, stab the dark brown back surface at a very shallow angle (nearly horizontal), pulling fibres upward into short loops. Work in rows from the base of the back toward the neck, with each loop about 3 to 5 mm long. Then needle the loops down at a slight rearward angle to create the illusion of rearward-pointing spines. The result should be a rough, bristled surface.
Apply two tiny black wool dots for the eyes and a small black triangle for the nose tip. Needle with the fine needle, using 4 to 5 firm strokes each.

attrs.intro: Two common problems with textured animal figures.

items.fix: Use the coarse needle (size 36) for spine texture. Fine needles smooth the surface; coarse needles disrupt it.
items.cause: The texture was made in the final fine-needling stage rather than in the coarse-needle stage.
items.symptom: Spine texture flattens back down during handling

items.fix: At the boundary, apply wisps of both colours overlapping slightly and needle through both layers with the fine needle. The mixed zone softens the transition.
items.cause: The two colours were applied in separate passes without blending at the border.
items.symptom: Colour boundary between cream and brown is too hard

attrs.heading: Common problems
